---
title: "Surrogate model performance"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
import json
import os
from pathlib import Path

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Results directory is passed via env var (set by `surrogate-mgem report`);
# defaults to the working directory the report is rendered in.
rd = Path(os.environ.get("SURROGATE_RESULTS_DIR", "."))
meta = json.loads((rd / "surrogate_meta.json").read_text())
metrics = json.loads((rd / "train_metrics.json").read_text())
preds = pd.read_csv(rd / "predictions.csv")
history = pd.read_csv(rd / "active_history.csv") if (rd / "active_history.csv").exists() else None
members = meta["target_names"]
```

## Summary

```{python}
# | echo: false
summary = {
    "community": metrics.get("community_id"),
    "mode": metrics.get("mode"),
    "members": metrics.get("n_members"),
    "test R²": round(metrics.get("r2_overall", float("nan")), 3),
    "test MAE": round(metrics.get("mae_overall", float("nan")), 4),
}
if metrics.get("mode") == "active":
    summary["seed → final train"] = f"{metrics['n_seed_samples']} → {metrics['n_final_train']}"
    summary["rounds"] = metrics["rounds"]
else:
    summary["train / test"] = f"{metrics['n_train']} / {metrics['n_test']}"
pd.DataFrame([summary]).T.rename(columns={0: "value"})
```

A negative R² means the surrogate is worse than predicting the mean growth —
usually a sign the sampled media carry too little signal (e.g. saturated
nutrient-rich media), not that the model is mis-specified.

## Predicted vs. true growth (held-out)

```{python}
# | echo: false
n = len(members)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, n, figsize=(4 * n, 4), squeeze=False)
for ax, g in zip(axes[0], members):
    sub = preds[preds["genome_id"] == g]
    ax.errorbar(sub["y_true"], sub["y_pred"], yerr=sub["y_std"], fmt="o", alpha=0.5, ms=4, elinewidth=0.7)
    lo = float(min(sub["y_true"].min(), sub["y_pred"].min()))
    hi = float(max(sub["y_true"].max(), sub["y_pred"].max()))
    ax.plot([lo, hi], [lo, hi], "k--", lw=1)
    r2 = metrics.get("r2_per_member", {}).get(g, float("nan"))
    ax.set_title(f"{g}\nR²={r2:.3f}")
    ax.set_xlabel("true growth")
    ax.set_ylabel("predicted growth")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Error bars are the ensemble's predictive std (epistemic uncertainty). Points far
off the diagonal with large bars are where the model knows it is unsure — the
regions the active loop targets.

## Residuals

```{python}
# | echo: false
preds["residual"] = preds["y_pred"] - preds["y_true"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
for g in members:
    ax.hist(preds.loc[preds["genome_id"] == g, "residual"], bins=20, alpha=0.5, label=g)
ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("predicted − true growth")
ax.set_ylabel("count")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
plt.show()
```

## Active-learning progress

```{python}
# | echo: false
if history is None:
    print("Static run — no active-learning history.")
else:
    fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))
    if "r2" in history:
        ax1.plot(history["n_train"], history["r2"], "o-")
        ax1.set_xlabel("training samples")
        ax1.set_ylabel("held-out R²")
        ax1.set_title("Generalisation vs. solve budget")
    if "mean_selected_uncertainty" in history:
        h = history.dropna(subset=["mean_selected_uncertainty"])
        ax2.plot(h["round"], h["mean_selected_uncertainty"], "o-", color="tab:orange")
        ax2.set_xlabel("round")
        ax2.set_ylabel("mean uncertainty of selected batch")
        ax2.set_title("Uncertainty of queried media")
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    history
```

The left panel should trend upward if spending solves where the ensemble is
uncertain actually improves held-out accuracy; the right panel should trend down
as the informative gaps get filled.
